text: The video displays a transverse cystotomy incision in the bladder, revealing the catheter balloon within the surgical field.
The surgeon places retraction sutures through the anterior abdominal wall, pulling back the anterolateral aspects of the bladder wall incision to improve surgical exposure.
The surgeon enters the bladder and makes a circumferential incision, visually demonstrating the extent of removal of the BPH adenoma.

The surgeon develops an incision creating a plane between the peripheral and transition zones of the prostate. Using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection, the large obstructing benign prostatic adenoma is excised. The dissection progresses from the posterior plane around to the lateral sides and continues anteriorly.
The primary surgeon coordinates with the assistant, who uses a retraction tool and a suction device to manage the surgical field during the prostatectomy.

The surgeon follows the anterior plane down towards the apex of the prostate using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques. Perforating blood vessels are encountered and controlled as they become visible during the dissection process.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the apex of the prostate, identifying the intraprostatic urethra beyond the adenoma. The adenoma is excised and removed, revealing a large central defect within the central cavity of the prostate.

The surgeon inserts a flow seal and a ratech to provide tamponade for hemostasis. Reconstruction begins with a V-lock suture, fashioning the posterior wall of the anastomosis by starting the suture line at the bladder and taking bites of the urethra.

The surgeon continues the anastomosis from the posterior aspect of the urethra, progressing through the lateral and anterior aspects of the incision to close the defect. Careful attention is given to identifying and preserving the ureteric orifices during the suture line, using a dissolvable V-lock suture.
The surgeon uses a second suture to complete the anastomosis, incorporating the anterior wall of the urethra and extending the suture line laterally to the patient's left.

The surgeon closes the large central defect within the prostate using an absorbable V-lock suture, achieving hemostasis. An indwelling catheter is inserted, and the transverse cystotomy is closed with the same type of suture.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the closed cystotomy site. A leak test is performed to check for any defects in the anastomosis, confirming the integrity of the closure before completing the procedure.
